command: sudo hping3 -c 1 -S -p 45001 target.example && sleep 1 && sudo hping3 -c 1 -A -p 45002 target.example && sleep 1 && sudo hping3 -c 1 -F -p 45003 target.example && ssh -p 22 user@target.example
explanation: Crafts a TCP knock sequence with specific flags (SYN, ACK, FIN) using hping3 to signal a port-unlock workflow on the remote device, then connects via SSH. Requires sudo for raw packet crafting. Detection: alert on hping3 invocation by non-admin users; IDS signatures for low-rate TCP probes with unusual flag combinations to high ports; monitor firewall for transient SSH ALLOW.
rationale_steps: Send crafted TCP packets with specific flags to designated knock ports (requires sudo).
Remote system validates the sequence and modifies rules to allow SSH.
Connect via SSH as soon as the rule opens.
Detect via EDR/IDS monitoring of hping3 usage and flag pattern anomalies to uncommon ports.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The chained commands use valid && separators with correct hping3 (-c, -S/-A/-F, -p) and ssh (-p) flags and no quoting or parsing issues.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It performs a realistic TCP knock sequence via specific TCP flags and ports using hping3 (with sudo) followed by an SSH attempt, aligning with T1205 traffic signaling behavior.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed with valid hping3 flags (-c, -S, -A, -F, -p), proper use of && chaining, sleep intervals, and a final ssh invocation with no syntax errors.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements a port-knocking traffic signaling sequence using hping3 to send TCP packets with specific flag combinations (SYN, ACK, FIN) to sequential high ports, which is a classic T1205 pattern for triggering a firewall rule to temporarily open SSH access before connecting.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command uses valid bash syntax, correctly chains commands with logical operators, and uses proper hping3 and ssh flags.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command perfectly implements a classic port knocking sequence using hping3 to send packets with specific TCP flags to predefined ports to trigger a firewall opening for SSH.
